fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The intrinsic path object on a type

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The intrinsic reflexive graph on a type A is a path object.
Univalence follows immediately by identification induction.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Path objects and the univalence axiom

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A reflexive graph \mathcal {A} \equiv \mathopen {}\left ({\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {},\approx _{\mathcal {A}}, \mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}}}\right )\mathclose {} is given by a type \mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {} of vertices together with a family of types \mathopen {}\left ({\approx _{\mathcal {A}}}\right )\mathclose {}\colon \mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {}\to \mathsf {Type} of edges and an assignment \mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}} : \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}} x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} x of reflexivity data to each vertex.

Let \mathcal {A} be a reflexive graph. A displayed reflexive graph \mathcal {B}\equiv \mathopen {}\left ({\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {B}}\right \rvert \mathclose {},\approx _{\mathcal {B}}^{\bullet },\mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {B}}^{\bullet }}\right )\mathclose {} over \mathcal {A} is given by families of types \mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {B}}\right \rvert \mathclose {}\colon \mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {Type} and \mathopen {}\left ({\approx _{\mathcal {B}}^{\bullet }}\right )\mathclose {} : \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left \{{x,y:\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\right \}\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left \{{x,y:\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\right \}\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left \{{x,y:\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\right \}\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left \{{x,y:\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\right \}\mathclose {}}} x \approx _{\mathcal {A}} y\to Bx \to By \to \mathsf {Type} together with an assignment of displayed reflexivity data \mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {B}}^{x}{u} : b\approx _{\mathcal {B}}^{\mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}}{x}}b for every u:Bx.

Given a displayed reflexive graph \mathcal {B} over \mathcal {A} and a vertex x:\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {}, we shall write \mathcal {B}\mathopen {}\left [{x}\right ]\mathclose {} for the component of \mathcal {B} at x:A, which we define to be the following reflexive graph:

Let \mathcal {A} be a reflexive graph, and let \mathcal {B} be a displayed reflexive graph over \mathcal {A}. The total reflexive graph of \mathcal {B} over \mathcal {A}, written \mathcal {A}.\mathcal {B}, is defined as follows:

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The intrinsic reflexive graph on a type

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The intrinsic reflexive graph with vertices in a type A, written \mathord {\vartriangle }A, is defined by setting x\approx _{\mathord {\vartriangle }A} y :\equiv \mathopen {}\left ({x = y}\right )\mathclose {} and \mathsf {rx}_{\mathord {\vartriangle }A}{x} :\equiv \mathsf {refl}.

Let \mathcal {A} be a reflexive graph, and let B\colon \mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {}\to \mathsf {Prop} be a family of propositions. Then the dependent sum projection \pi _1\colon \mathopen {}\left ({\mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}Bx}\right )\mathclose {}\to A creates a reflexive graph structure \mathopen {}\left \{{x:\mathcal {A}\,\middle \vert \,Bx}\right \}\mathclose {} on \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}Bx as follows:
Let \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B} be two reflexive graphs. We define the binary product of \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B} to be the following reflexive graph:

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Product of a family of reflexive graphs

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {B} be a family of reflexive graphs indexed in a type A. We define the product \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathcal {B} x of the family \mathcal {B} to be the following reflexive graph:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} \mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathcal {B} x}\right \rvert \mathclose {} &:\equiv \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {B} x}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \\ f \approx _{\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathcal {B} x} g &:\equiv \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}} f x \approx _{\mathcal {B} x} g x \\ \mathsf {rx}_{\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathcal {B} x}{f} &:\equiv \lambda {x}\mathpunct {.}\mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {B} x}\mathopen {}\left ({fx}\right )\mathclose {} \end {aligned}

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A reflexive graph \mathcal {A} is called univalent when for all x:\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {} the type \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({y:\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({y:\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({y:\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({y:\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}} x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y is a proposition, or (equivalently), is contractible with centre \mathopen {}\left ({x,\mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}}{x}}\right )\mathclose {}. We shall refer to a univalent reflexive graph as a path object.
We will write \mathsf {PathObj} for the (large) type of path objects. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given path object to its underlying type of vertices.

Let \mathcal {A} be a path object, and let B\colon \mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {}\to \mathsf {Prop} be a family of propositions. Then the reflexive subgraph comprehension \mathopen {}\left \{{x:\mathcal {A}\,\middle \vert \,B\mathopen {}\left ({x}\right )\mathclose {}}\right \}\mathclose {} is a path object.
Univalence follows from univalence of \mathcal {A} and the fact that B is valued in propositions.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The intrinsic path object on a type

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The intrinsic reflexive graph on a type A is a path object.
Univalence follows immediately by identification induction.
The reflexive graph structure on types given by equivalences is univalent. We shall write \mathsf {Type} to indicate the resulting (large) path object of types and equivalences.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The univalence principle for dependent functions

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The reflexive graph structure on \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{Bx} by homotopy and identity homotopies is univalent.

Given a dependent function f : \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{Bx}, if each component Bx is contractible with centre fx then the dependent product \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{Bx} is contractible with centre f.
This follows immediately from univalence for functions.
The dependent product of a family of propositions is a proposition.

Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} be a family of propositions; fixing f : \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{Bx}, it suffices to show any other g :\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}Bx may be identified with f. As B is a family of propositions, we know that each Bx is contractible with centre fx. Therefore, the closure of contractible types under dependent products () shows that \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}Bx is contractible with centre f.
The product of a family \mathcal {B}\colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} path objects is a path object.
Univalence follows from the closure of families of contractible types under dependent products () via the distributivity of \prod over \sum .
Let \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B} be two path objects; then the binary product \mathcal {A}\times \mathcal {B} is a path object.
The univalence property of \mathcal {A}\times \mathcal {B} follows immediately from that of \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B}.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Coherent remainder as a path object

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let f\colon A\to B be a function equipped with a section \sigma \equiv \mathopen {}\left ({g\colon B\to A,\epsilon \colon f\circ g = 1_{B}}\right )\mathclose {}. We can equip the coherent remainder of \sigma with the structure of a path object as follows:

fr:ol.fr:li: We equip g\circ f\sim 1_{A} with the following dependent product of intrinsic path object structures: \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}} \mathord {\vartriangle } \mathopen {}\left ({ g\mathopen {}\left ({f x}\right )\mathclose {} = x }\right )\mathclose {}

For each \eta \colon g\circ f\sim 1_{A}, we likewise equip \mathsf {act}_{f}\circ \eta \sim \epsilon \circ f with the following dependent product of intrinsic path object structures: \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}} \mathord {\vartriangle } \mathopen {}\left ({ \mathsf {act}_{f}\mathopen {}\left ({\eta x}\right )\mathclose {} = \epsilon \mathopen {}\left ({f x}\right )\mathclose {} }\right )\mathclose {}

fr:li.fr:p: We exhibit the latter as a right-cloven family of path objects over the former with the cleaving that for \eta _0,\eta _1\colon g\circ f\sim 1_{A} and \eta _{01}\colon \eta _0 \sim \eta _1 defines \eta _{01}^*\colon \mathsf {act}_{f}\circ \eta _1\sim \epsilon \circ f \to \mathsf {act}_{f}\circ \eta _0\sim \epsilon \circ f as follows:

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: With this cleaving in mind, finally define \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma to be the following contravariant sum of path objects:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma :\equiv \mathchoice {\textstyle \int _{\mathopen {}\left ({ \eta : \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}} \mathord {\vartriangle } \mathopen {}\left ({g\mathopen {}\left ({f x}\right )\mathclose {}=x}\right )\mathclose {} }\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \int _{\mathopen {}\left ({ \eta : \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}} \mathord {\vartriangle } \mathopen {}\left ({g\mathopen {}\left ({f x}\right )\mathclose {}=x}\right )\mathclose {} }\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \int _{\mathopen {}\left ({ \eta : \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}} \mathord {\vartriangle } \mathopen {}\left ({g\mathopen {}\left ({f x}\right )\mathclose {}=x}\right )\mathclose {} }\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \int _{\mathopen {}\left ({ \eta : \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}} \mathord {\vartriangle } \mathopen {}\left ({g\mathopen {}\left ({f x}\right )\mathclose {}=x}\right )\mathclose {} }\right )\mathclose {}}} \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}} \mathord {\vartriangle } \mathopen {}\left ({ \mathsf {act}_{f}\mathopen {}\left ({\eta x}\right )\mathclose {} = \epsilon \mathopen {}\left ({f x}\right )\mathclose {} }\right )\mathclose {}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Given elements \mathopen {}\left ({\eta _0,\chi _0}\right )\mathclose {} and \mathopen {}\left ({\eta _1,\chi _1}\right )\mathclose {} of \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma , the resulting type \mathopen {}\left ({\eta _0,\chi _0}\right )\mathclose {}\cong _{\mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma }\mathopen {}\left ({\eta _1,\chi _1}\right )\mathclose {} is definitionally equivalent to the following dependent sum:

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Path object structure on reflexive graphs

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: We may define a path object structure on the type of reflexive graphs with vertices in a given type A, so that an isomorphism from \mathcal {G} to \mathcal {G}' is given by a pair of a pointwise equivalence of types H\colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathopen {}\left ({x\approx _{\mathcal {G}}y}\right )\mathclose {}\cong \mathopen {}\left ({x\approx _{\mathcal {G}'}y}\right )\mathclose {} that preserves reflexivity data as witnessed by a dependent homotopy I\colon \mathopen {}\left ({\lambda {x}\mathpunct {.} H\,x\,x\,\mathopen {}\left ({\mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {G}}{x}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {} \sim \mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {G}'}.

We first show that the dependent product of intrinsic path objects \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathord {\vartriangle }\mathopen {}\left ({-xx}\right )\mathclose {} is a left-cloven over the path object \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathsf {Type}: given an equivalence H : \mathopen {}\left ({\approx _{\mathcal {G}}}\right )\mathclose {}\approx _{\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathsf {Type}} \mathopen {}\left ({\approx _{\mathcal {G}'}}\right )\mathclose {} of graphs and a reflexivity datum r\colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}} x\approx _{\mathcal {G}} x, we define H_*r x :\equiv H\,x\,x\,\mathopen {}\left ({rx}\right )\mathclose {}; the unit law holds definitionally.

Therefore, we may form the covariant sum \mathchoice {\textstyle \oint _{\mathopen {}\left ({E:\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathsf {Type}}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \oint _{\mathopen {}\left ({E:\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathsf {Type}}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \oint _{\mathopen {}\left ({E:\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathsf {Type}}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \oint _{\mathopen {}\left ({E:\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathsf {Type}}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathord {\vartriangle }\mathopen {}\left ({Exx}\right )\mathclose {}, in which equivalences are given by homotopies of reflexivity data over graph equivalences.

Given f\colon A\to B and b:B, we shall impose a path object structure on the fiber \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}fx=b of f over b. The family \mathord {\vartriangle }\mathopen {}\left ({f-=b}\right )\mathclose {}\colon A\to \mathsf {PathObj} given by intrinsic path objects in each component is right-cloven over \mathord {\vartriangle }A:

fr:ol.fr:li: for p\colon x = y and \phi :fy=b, we define p^*b\colon fx=b to be identification \mathsf {act}_{f}p\cdot \phi ;
the unit law can be seen to hold using the unit laws for composition of identification.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Therefore, we may compute the contravariant sum \mathchoice {\textstyle \int _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:\mathord {\vartriangle }A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \int _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:\mathord {\vartriangle }A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \int _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:\mathord {\vartriangle }A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \int _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:\mathord {\vartriangle }A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\mathord {\vartriangle }\mathopen {}\left ({fx=b}\right )\mathclose {}} using the cleaving above, with \mathopen {}\left ({x,\phi }\right )\mathclose {} \approx _{\mathchoice {\textstyle \int _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:\mathord {\vartriangle }A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \int _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:\mathord {\vartriangle }A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \int _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:\mathord {\vartriangle }A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \int _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:\mathord {\vartriangle }A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\mathord {\vartriangle }\mathopen {}\left ({fx=b}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathopen {}\left ({y,\psi }\right )\mathclose {} definitionally equal to \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({p: x= y}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({p: x= y}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({p: x= y}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({p: x= y}\right )\mathclose {}}} \phi = \mathsf {act}_{f}p\cdot \psi .
It is a proposition that a given type A is contractible.

Suppose we have two centres of contraction a,a':A with \chi _\bullet \colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}a = x and \chi '_\bullet \colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}a'=x as witnesses of contraction. To identify \mathopen {}\left ({a,\chi _\bullet }\right )\mathclose {} with \mathopen {}\left ({a',\chi '_\bullet }\right )\mathclose {}, we employ the covariant sum of each of the pointwise path object structures on \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}-=x over the intrinsic path object structure on A, constructing an identification p\colon a = a' followed by a homotopy H \colon \mathopen {}\left ({\mathopen {}\left ({\chi _{a'}}\right )\mathclose {}_*^{\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}-=x} \chi _\bullet }\right )\mathclose {} \sim \chi '_\bullet . Fixing z\colon A, we compute:
The first and second steps follow from general computation lemmas for transport, and the last step follows from the fact that A is a set, which we may deduce from and .

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The intrinsic left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Given a type A and a family of path objects \mathcal {B}\colon A\to \mathsf {PathObj}, we can always define a left cleaving of \mathcal {B} over the intrinsic path object \mathord {\vartriangle }A. The left cleaving is obtained directly from the transport operation of the identification type.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The intrinsic reflexive graph on a type

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The intrinsic reflexive graph with vertices in a type A, written \mathord {\vartriangle }A, is defined by setting x\approx _{\mathord {\vartriangle }A} y :\equiv \mathopen {}\left ({x = y}\right )\mathclose {} and \mathsf {rx}_{\mathord {\vartriangle }A}{x} :\equiv \mathsf {refl}.

A reflexive graph \mathcal {A} is called univalent when for all x:\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {} the type \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({y:\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({y:\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({y:\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{\mathopen {}\left ({y:\mathopen {}\left \lvert {\mathcal {A}}\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}} x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y is a proposition, or (equivalently), is contractible with centre \mathopen {}\left ({x,\mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}}{x}}\right )\mathclose {}. We shall refer to a univalent reflexive graph as a path object.
We will write \mathsf {PathObj} for the (large) type of path objects. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given path object to its underlying type of vertices.
